subject: Internal medicine
title: Upper respiratory tract infection
preambleMarkdown:
# Upper respiratory tract infection

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Upper respiratory tract infection is an infectious syndrome or organism-related topic in internal medicine.
- This article focuses on adult presentation, differential diagnosis, diagnostics, complications, chronic management, and prevention.
- The practical clinical priority is host-pathogen interaction, source control, and timely antimicrobial decisions.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Upper respiratory tract infection to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Upper respiratory tract infection is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is host-pathogen interaction, source control, and timely antimicrobial decisions.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Community-acquired, healthcare-associated, travel-related, device-related, or opportunistic infection.
- Localized, disseminated, recurrent, latent, or postinfectious presentation.
- Bacterial, viral, fungal, parasitic, toxin-mediated, or mixed infection.
- Uncomplicated disease, severe disease, sepsis, source-control need, or outbreak/public-health concern.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Upper respiratory tract infection depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In internal medicine, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Upper respiratory tract infection.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Upper respiratory tract infection disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Upper respiratory tract infection should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Upper respiratory tract infection first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Upper respiratory tract infection, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Fever, localizing symptoms, exposure history, immune status, device or procedure history, and severity define the syndrome.
- Immunocompromised patients, neonates, pregnancy, and older adults may lack classic symptoms.
- Sepsis, airway threat, neurologic involvement, necrosis, and rapidly progressive disease change urgency.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Upper respiratory tract infection, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused internal medicine examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Upper respiratory tract infection presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Upper respiratory tract infection with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Upper respiratory tract infection is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Upper respiratory tract infection should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize sepsis, obtain high-yield cultures when they will not delay care, and start empiric therapy when indicated.
- Narrow therapy once organism, susceptibility, source, and response are known.
- Add source control, isolation, prophylaxis, vaccination, contact tracing, or public-health notification when relevant.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Upper respiratory tract infection is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses host-pathogen interaction, source control, and timely antimicrobial decisions.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Start empiric antimicrobial therapy promptly when severe infection, sepsis, meningitis, necrotizing infection, or immunocompromised infection is suspected.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Drain abscesses, remove infected devices, debride necrotic tissue, decompress obstruction, or obtain diagnostic samples when source control is needed.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Upper respiratory tract infection, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Upper respiratory tract infection may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Upper respiratory tract infection patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Upper respiratory tract infection, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Upper respiratory tract infection in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Upper respiratory tract infection, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Upper respiratory tract infection does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Upper respiratory tract infection may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Upper respiratory tract infection, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Upper respiratory tract infection as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Do not delay antimicrobials in unstable sepsis.
- Cultures are most valuable before antibiotics when feasible, but stabilization comes first.
- Source control can matter as much as antimicrobial choice.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Upper respiratory tract infection draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

### Source Excerpt: Upper Respiratory Tract Infections

_Source lines: 13772-14139._

```text
V. UPPER RESPIRATORY TRACT INFECTIONS
A. Streptococcal pharyngitis
1. Discussion. Pharyngitis (infection of the pharynx and tonsils) is commonly
caused by viruses and bacteria. Group A !-hemolytic streptococci are clearly
the most important causative agents, accounting for half of all pharyn-
F I G U R E
6-4 	Herpes genital lesions.

(Courtesy of Lawrence B. Stack, MD.)

I N F E C T I O U S D I S E A S E E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	169

Infectious Disease Emergencies
geal infections in patients between the ages of 5 and 15 years (see Clinical
Pearl 6-5).

2. Clinical features. Although no set of symptoms is classic for streptococcal
pharyngitis, the sudden onset of a fever and sore throat with enlargement of
the cervical lymph nodes and red, swollen tonsils and palate are common. The
presence of a scarlatiniform rash in the presence of pharyngitis practically iden-
tifies a group A "-hemolytic streptococcus as the causative agent. Headache,
vomiting, abdominal pain, meningismus, and torticollis can occur as well.

3. Differential diagnoses include viral illnesses, diphtheria (Corynebacterium diph-
theriae infection), N. gonorrhoeae infection, and Epstein-Barr virus infection.

a. Pharyngeal diphtheria is characterized by tissue necrosis and the creation
of a pseudomembrane over the tonsils, soft palate, uvula, or pharyngeal
wall that can lead to airway obstruction (see V.B).

b. Pharyngeal gonorrhea. N. gonorrhoeae causes a mild or asymptomatic
pharyngitis seen in some sexually active adults and victims of child abuse
(see IV.A.2.a.iii).

c. Infectious mononucleosis. Epstein-Barr virus usually causes asymptomatic
infections early in childhood and adolescence but has been associated
with isolated tonsillopharyngitis characterized by malaise, fatigue, and
sore throat. There is an increase in the number of atypical lymphocytes
on peripheral blood smear and the heterophil antibody is present in over
90% of patients older than 5 years. Infectious mononucleosis is generally a
benign, self-limited, somewhat prolonged illness.

## 4. Evaluation

a. Throat culture is the mainstay of diagnosis.

b. Serology. Rapid streptococcal antigen sensitivity of 50% to 90% and specificity
of 98% to 100% when compared with throat culture (the gold standard).

5. Therapy. The objectives of management are to prevent rheumatic fever and
suppurative complications and to hasten recovery.

a. Antibiotic therapy
i. A single dose of intramuscular penicillin G benzathine (600,000 to
1.2 million U for an average-sized adult) is very effective.

ii. Oral penicillin V (250 mg four times daily for 10 days) is equally
effective.

iii. First- and second-generation cephalosporins and erythromycin are
popular alternatives for patients who are allergic to penicillin.

b. Symptomatic therapy includes acetaminophen, over-the-counter throat
sprays and lozenges, and warm fluids.

6. Disposition. Pharyngitis is managed primarily on an outpatient basis.

B. Diphtheria is an acute infectious disease characterized by the formation of a
fibrinous pseudomembrane on the respiratory mucosa. Cutaneous diphtheria
lesions are also common.

## 1. Discussion

a. Etiology. Diphtheria is caused by C. diphtheriae.

b. Pathogenesis. Transmission is via the secretions of infected individuals or
carriers or via contaminated fomites.

Centor criteria, used to diagnose streptococcal pharyngitis:
1. Tonsillar exudate
2. Fever or history of fever !38C
3. Tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy
4. Absence of cough
0-2 criteria: antibiotic treatment generally not indicated
3-4 criteria: consider antibiotic treatment
PEARL	CLINICAL 	6-5
170 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Infectious Disease Emergencies
c. Complications
i. Myocarditis (days 10 to 14), congestive heart failure, and arrhythmias
ii. Peripheral nerve palsies (weeks 3 to 6), bilateral flaccid weakness or
paralysis, decreased deep tendon reflexes, ptosis, strabismus, and the
inability to accommodate
d. Predisposing factors. Crowded habitats and low socioeconomic status pre-
dispose to the spread of diphtheria. The most significant risk factor is inad-
equate immunization.

2. Clinical features
a. Symptoms. Patients complain of a sore throat, dysphagia, and fever accom-
panied by nausea, vomiting, chills, and a headache. The patient's breathing
may be labored as a result of upper airway edema or sudden detachment of
the pseudomembrane, leading to sudden respiratory obstruction.

b. Physical examination findings include tachycardia and the pseudomem-
brane. Attempts to remove the pseudomembrane usually result in bleeding.

3. Differential diagnoses include streptococcal pharyngitis, viral pharyngitis,
gonococcal pharyngitis, oral syphilis, epiglottitis, and (in the late stages of
disease) Guillain-Barre syndrome.

4. Evaluation. The diagnosis is largely based on the clinical appearance of the
membrane.

a. Culture. If antibiotic therapy has not been initiated, growth on Loeffler agar
will reveal an aerobic, motile organism in 8 to 12 hours.

b. Gram staining of the membrane may show gram-positive, club-shaped rods.

5. Therapy. Spontaneous reversal occurs slowly over many weeks, but prompt treat-
ment is essential to avoid complications. Recovery is slow, and too rapid a return
to even "normal" levels of physical activity may worsen toxin-induced myocarditis.

a. Diphtheria antitoxin is derived from horse serum (therefore, sensitivity
testing is recommended) and administered intramuscularly to neutralize the
toxin. Antitoxin neutralizes only that toxin that is not yet bound to cells
and should be administered immediately, without waiting for culture results.

b. Antibacterial therapy. Penicillin G eliminates the carrier state but has not
been shown to alter the course, complications, or outcome of diphtheria.

Erythromycin, ampicillin, clindamycin, and rifampin may be useful.

c. Supportive therapy. If oxygenation is impaired or obstruction appears
imminent secondary to dislodgement of the membrane, tracheostomy is
preferred over endotracheal intubation.

6. Disposition. Patients require immediate admission to the ICU.

## 7. Prevention

a. Contacts of patients with negative cultures and no symptoms should main-
tain adequate immunization status.

b. Asymptomatic carriers (those with positive cultures but no symptoms)
should be treated with a full course of antibiotics and isolation at home.

c. Symptomatic carriers should be treated with antibiotics and antitoxin.

C. Epiglottitis
1. Discussion. Inflammatory disorders of the laryngeal region may be either
supraglottic (i.e., affecting the supraglottis or epiglottis) or infraglottic
(i.e., affecting the larynx or trachea).

a. Etiology. Epiglottitis can result from:
i. Chemical damage (e.g., aspiration of gasoline)
ii. Mechanical injury (e.g., trauma, burns)
iii. Sarcoidosis (causes chronic epiglottitis)
iv. Infection
a) Viruses (e.g., parainfluenza virus, adenovirus, respiratory syncytial
virus, herpes virus)
b) Bacteria (e.g., H. influenzae, group A " -hemolytic streptococci,
S. pneumoniae, Haemophilus parainfluenzae, Klebsiella pneumoniae,
Fusobacterium necrophorum, Pasteurella multocida)
c) Fungi (e.g., Aspergillus, Candida )
Quick 	HIT
The dosage of the antitoxin
is based on the patient's
clinical presentation, not on
age or weight.

I N F E C T I O U S D I S E A S E E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	171

Infectious Disease Emergencies
2. Clinical features. Epiglottitis is characterized by a sudden, fulminant course.

a. Patient history. Usually, there is no history of a prodromal upper respiratory
tract infection. (This is in contrast to what is normally observed in patients
with croup.)

b. Symptoms include a disproportionately sore throat, fever, a muffled
voice (versus the hoarseness seen in croup; see Chapter 15.V.A), minimal
coughing (versus the bark-like cough seen in croup), dysphagia, and respi-
ratory distress.

c. Physical examination findings include:
i. Drooling, dyspnea, tachypnea, inspiratory stridor (softer and less prom-
inent than that seen with croup), and use of the accessory respiratory
muscles
ii. Cervical adenopathy
iii. Tripod position (patient leans forward, supporting him- or herself with
both hands, and hyperextends his or her neck)
iv. Toxic or septic appearance
3. Differential diagnoses include croup, sepsis, aspirated foreign body, periton-
sillar or retropharyngeal abscess, diphtheria, lingual tonsillitis, angioedema,
pharyngitis, drug allergy, inhalation or ingestion of toxic substances, acute
thyroiditis, and epiglottic hematoma.

4. Evaluation. Examination of the pharynx and administration of sedatives
should be avoided when evaluating a patient with suspected epiglottitis.

a. Laryngoscopy can be used to view the pharynx and larynx in adults.

i. Indications. Oral instrumentation (e.g., indirect laryngoscopy) is indi-
cated in the following circumstances:
a) Patients in whom epiglottitis is only suspected and with mild to
moderate signs and symptoms
b) Patients without any indicators of potential airway compromise
ii. Extreme caution should be employed. The procedure should be per-
formed only in EDs with immediately accessible complete resuscitation
equipment by a physician experienced in laryngoscopy.

b. Radiography. A portable lateral neck radiograph is adequate for the diag-
nosis of epiglottitis and is much safer than allowing the patient to leave
the ED. Most radiographic studies are inconclusive and should not be per-
formed until experts on airway intervention are onsite. However, soft tissue
lateral neck radiographs may demonstrate enlargement of the epiglottis
(greater than 8 mm) and aryepiglottic folds (greater than 7 mm) and/or
ballooning of the hypopharynx with air (Figure 6-5).

Quick 	HIT
No patient in respiratory dis-
tress or with any indication
of potential airway compro-
mise should ever be trans-
ferred to another institution
until the airway is protected.

F I G U R E
6-5 	Radiograph of epiglottis "thumbprint."

172 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Infectious Disease Emergencies

## 5. Therapy

a. Intubation (in a controlled setting, ideally in the operating room, by an
anesthesiologist, otolaryngologist, or pediatric surgeon) is necessary.

i. Venipuncture, injections, arterial blood gas analysis, the use of oxygen
masks, and the taking of radiographs should be avoided until the patient
is successfully intubated.

ii. Blind nasal or orotracheal intubation should never be attempted.

b. Administration of racemic epinephrine is contraindicated.

c. Antibiotic therapy should be initiated immediately after blood and epiglot-
tic cultures are taken and successful intubation has occurred.

i. The antibiotic of choice is cefotaxime (50 mg/kg every 8 hours) or
ceftriaxone (50 mg/kg/day administered intravenously in four equal
doses).

ii. As an alternative, ampicillin (200 mg/kg/day administered intravenously
in four equal doses) together with chloramphenicol (100 mg/kg/day
administered intravenously in four equal doses) can be used until the
results of cultures and sensitivities are available.

d. Surgical intervention. Bedside cricothyrotomy or tracheostomy may be
necessary. Transtracheal needle jet ventilation in children younger than
8 years old may be done as a temporizing measure until the tracheostomy
can be performed in the operating room.

6. Disposition. Laryngoscopy should be repeated prior to extubation, which is
usually accomplished in 24 to 48 hours with appropriate treatment. ICU moni-
toring is indicated for 24 hours following extubation.

## 7. Prevention

a. Rifampin prophylaxis (20 mg/kg/day once daily for 4 days, maximum
600 mg/day) is indicated for all family members and household and
daycare contacts.

b. Vaccination against H. influenzae is an excellent prophylactic measure but
obviously is not 100% effective.

D. Laryngitis
1. Discussion. Laryngitis in the adult is usually synonymous with hoarseness.

a. Etiology
i. Acute laryngitis. Causes of acute laryngitis include:
a) Trauma (e.g., laryngeal hematoma, edema secondary to exposure to
heated gasses or liquids, dislocation or disruption of an arytenoid)
b) Infection
1) Viruses (e.g., adenoviruses, coronaviruses, parainfluenza viruses,
respiratory syncytial viruses)
2) Bacteria (e.g., Staphylococcus, H. influenzae, C. diphtheriae,
mycobacteria)
3) Fungi
ii. Chronic laryngitis
a) Intralaryngeal causes include benign laryngeal disease (e.g., benign
polyps, vocal fatigue) and malignant laryngeal disease.

b) Extralaryngeal causes
1) Perilaryngeal causes include infiltrating thyroid carcinoma,
lymphoma, and deep neck infection.

2) Remote causes include recurrent entrapment from lung cancer
and stroke.

3) Systemic causes include neuromuscular disorders, rheumatoid
disorders, and infiltrative processes.

2. Clinical features. Hoarseness is the most common manifestation of laryngeal
disease regardless of its cause.

a. Symptoms include dysphagia, odynophagia, and hoarseness. If the
hoarseness presents as a part of the self-limited coryza syndrome (the most
common form of adult laryngitis), it usually develops 2 to 4 days into the
illness and lasts from 1 to 3 weeks.

I N F E C T I O U S D I S E A S E E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	173

Infectious Disease Emergencies
b. Physical examination findings may include stridor; aspiration; a gray
pseudomembrane attached to the posterior pharynx; lesions on the nose,
pharynx, larynx, skin, penis, vagina, or bladder; peripheral neuritis or
cranial nerve dysfunction; and signs of myocarditis (e.g., heart failure,
dysrhythmias).

3. Differential diagnoses
a. Epiglottitis. Laryngitis must be differentiated immediately from epiglottitis.

i. Clinically, findings such as the tripod position, drooling, reluctance to
speak, extreme apprehension, stridor, and retractions clearly indicate
impending airway obstruction.

ii. Radiographic findings may help (see V.D.4.b).

b. Aspiration

## 4. Evaluation

a. Laboratory studies are generally not helpful diagnostically but may be useful
for baseline and follow-up. Diphtheria may be an exception (see V.B.4).

b. Radiography. Compared to epiglottitis, laryngitis calls for no specific radio-
logic testing.

## 5. Therapy

a. Coryza syndrome. Treatment is symptomatic and includes voice rest.

b. Staphylococcus or H. influenzae infection. Treatment with a third-generation
cephalosporin is effective.

## 6. Disposition

a. Discharge. Most patients are discharged for follow-up with their family
physician. Patients with hoarseness persisting more than 4 to 6 weeks
should be referred to an otorhinolaryngologist for further evaluation.

b. Admission. Patients with a clinical presentation suggestive of impending
airway obstruction are admitted to the hospital and treated as described
for epiglottitis.
```
